  • Time-Stepping for Non-Smooth Mechanical Systems

Time-Stepping for Non-Smooth Mechanical Systems

This chapter details the discrete equations used in Cardillo’s Moreau-style time-stepping integrator for rigid bodies subject to unilateral constraints and friction [1]. The integrator employs a Velocity-level splitting scheme with a generalized \(\theta\)-method for velocity updates.

Non-smooth mechanical systems are characterized by abrupt velocity changes and impulsive reactions. The Moreau time-stepping scheme integrates the system at the velocity level, which naturally accommodates impulsive constraints while maintaining stability during smooth phases.

Symbols and Notation

  • \(\mathbf{M}\): Block-diagonal mass/inertia matrix.

  • \(\mathbf{u}\): Stacked generalized velocity vector.

  • \(\mathbf{W}_g, \mathbf{W}_\gamma\): Constraint Jacobian blocks (spring and damper rows).

  • \(\mathbf{W}_{NT}\): Contact Jacobian (stacked normal and tangential rows).

  • \(\mathbf{\Lambda}_{NT}\): Accumulated contact impulses over the step.

  • \(\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{NT}^+\): Post-impact contact velocity in Normal and Tangential directions.

  • \(\mathbf{f}^{\mathrm{ext}}\): External forces (e.g. gravity).

  • \(\mathbf{v}_g, \mathbf{v}_\gamma\): Velocity-source terms from trajectory-driven bodies.

  • \(\mathbf{C}, \mathbf{A}\): Compliance/attenuation diagonals.

  • \(\boldsymbol\lambda_g, \boldsymbol\lambda_\gamma\): Lagrange multiplier densities.

  • \(\mathbf{\Lambda}_g, \mathbf{\Lambda}_\gamma\): Solver-scaled impulses, defined as \(\mathbf{\Lambda}_g = -\Delta t\,\boldsymbol\lambda_g\) and \(\mathbf{\Lambda}_\gamma = -\Delta t\,\boldsymbol\lambda_{\gamma}\).

  • \(\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{\text{stab}}\): Baumgarte position-correction term.

  • \(\theta, \Delta t\): Integration parameter and time-step size.

Continuous Equations of Motion

The system is governed by the following measure-differential equations:

\[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} \dot{\mathbf{q}} &= \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{u} \\ \mathbf{M} \operatorname{d}\!{\mathbf{u}} &= \bigl[\mathbf{f}^\mathrm{ext}(t, \mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{f}^\mathrm{gyr}(\mathbf{u})\bigr] \operatorname{d}\!t + \mathbf{W}_g(\mathbf{q}) \operatorname{d}\!{\boldsymbol{\pi}_g} + \mathbf{W}_\gamma(\mathbf{q}) \operatorname{d}\!{\boldsymbol{\pi}_\gamma} + \mathbf{W}_N(\mathbf{q}) \operatorname{d}\!\boldsymbol{\pi}_N + \mathbf{W}_T(\mathbf{q}) \operatorname{d}\!\boldsymbol{\pi}_T \\ \mathbf{0} &= \mathbf{C} \dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}}_g + \mathbf{W}_g^\top(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{u} \\ \mathbf{0} &= \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\lambda}_\gamma + \mathbf{W}_\gamma^\top(\mathbf{q}) \mathbf{u} \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Velocity-Level Splitting

The time step is decomposed into three phases using an intermediate configuration \(\mathbf{q}_{n+\theta}\).

1. First Phase (Explicit Position Update): Updates configuration based on the current velocity:

\[\mathbf{q}_{n+\theta} = \mathbf{q}_n + (1-\theta)\,\Delta t\,\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{q}_n)\,\mathbf{u}_n\]

2. Second Phase (Implicit Velocity Update) at \(n+\theta\): The momentum balance is solved using a generalized \(\theta\)-method.

Note

While \(\theta=0.5\) is often desired for second-order accuracy, choosing \(\theta \in (0.5, 1]\) introduces numerical damping, which improves stability for stiff mechanical systems (e.g., beam elements).

Approximating the gyroscopic term as \(\mathbf{f}^{\mathrm{gyr}} \approx \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{u}_n)\mathbf{u}_{n+1}\) and defining \(\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{eff}} := \mathbf{M} - \Delta t\,\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{u}_n)\), we use the backward Euler approximation for impulses:

\[\Delta \boldsymbol{\pi}_{g,n+1} = -\Delta t\,\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{g,n+1}, \quad \Delta \boldsymbol{\pi}_{\gamma,n+1} = -\Delta t\,\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{\gamma,n+1}.\]

The discrete momentum equation becomes:

\[\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{eff}}\mathbf{u}_{n+1} = \mathbf{M}\mathbf{u}_n + \Delta t\,\mathbf{f}^{\mathrm{ext}}_{n+\theta} + \mathbf{W}_{NT}\,\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{NT} + \mathbf{W}_g \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_g + \mathbf{W}_\gamma \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_\gamma\]

3. Third Phase (Second Drift): Updates configuration for the next time step:

\[\mathbf{q}_{n+1} = \mathbf{q}_{n+\theta} + \theta\,\Delta t\,\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{q}_{n+\theta})\,\mathbf{u}_{n+1}\]

Discrete Constraint and Contact Laws

The constraint evolution laws evaluated at \(n+\theta\) are:

\[\mathbf{C}\,(\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{g,n+1}-\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{g,n}) + \Delta t\,\mathbf{W}_{g}^\top\bigl((1-\theta)\,\mathbf{u}_n + \theta\,\mathbf{u}_{n+1}\bigr) = \mathbf{0}\]
\[\mathbf{A}\,\boldsymbol{\lambda}_{\gamma,n+1} + \mathbf{W}_{\gamma}^\top\bigl((1-\theta)\,\mathbf{u}_n + \theta\,\mathbf{u}_{n+1}\bigr) = \mathbf{0}\]

For contact constraints with friction, the following complementarity/feasibility conditions apply at each contact \(j\):

\[\begin{split}\begin{aligned} &0 \le \gamma_{N,j}^+ \ \perp\ \Lambda_{N,j} \ge 0, \\ &\lVert\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{T,j}\rVert \le \mu_j\,\Lambda_{N,j}, \\ &\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{T,j}=\mathbf{0}\ \Rightarrow\ \lVert\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{T,j}\rVert \le \mu_j\,\Lambda_{N,j}\qquad\text{(sticking)}, \\ &\boldsymbol{\gamma}^+_{T,j}\neq\mathbf{0}\ \Rightarrow\ \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{T,j} = -\mu_j\,\Lambda_{N,j}\,\dfrac{\boldsymbol{\gamma}^+_{T,j}}{\lVert\boldsymbol{\gamma}^+_{T,j}\rVert} \quad\text{and}\quad \lVert\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{T,j}\rVert = \mu_j\,\Lambda_{N,j}\qquad\text{(sliding)}. \end{aligned}\end{split}\]

Scaled Block Linear System

Using the Baumgarte stabilization term \(\boldsymbol{\gamma}_{\text{stab}} = (\mathbf{C} \boldsymbol{\lambda}_{g,n} + \mathbf{g}(t, \mathbf{q})) \cdot \frac{\beta}{\Delta t \, \theta}\), the discrete equations can be rearranged into the following scaled block linear system:

\[\begin{split}\underbrace{% \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{eff}} & \mathbf{W}_g & \mathbf{W}_{\gamma} \\ \mathbf{W}_g^\top & -\dfrac{1}{\theta\,\Delta t^2}\mathbf{C} & \mathbf{0} \\ \mathbf{W}_{\gamma}^\top & \mathbf{0} & -\dfrac{1}{\theta\,\Delta t}\mathbf{A} \end{pmatrix}% }_{\mathcal{S}(\mathbf{q}_{n+\theta},\Delta t,\theta)}\; \underbrace{% \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{u}_{n+1} \\ \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{g,n+1} \\ \boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{\gamma,n+1} \end{pmatrix}% }_{\mathbf{x}_{n+1}} = \underbrace{% \begin{pmatrix} \mathbf{M}\mathbf{u}_n + \Delta t\,\mathbf{f}^{\mathrm{ext}}_{n+\theta} + \mathbf{W}_{NT}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{NT} \\ -\dfrac{C\,\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{g,n}}{\theta\,\Delta t^2} - \dfrac{1-\theta}{\theta}\mathbf{W}_g^\top \mathbf{u}_n - \dfrac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{v}_g + \boldsymbol{\gamma}_{\text{stab}} \\ -\dfrac{1-\theta}{\theta}\mathbf{W}_\gamma^\top \mathbf{u}_n - \dfrac{1}{\theta}\mathbf{v}_\gamma \end{pmatrix}% }_{\mathbf{b}_n(\mathbf{q}_{n+\theta},\mathbf{x}_n,\Delta t,\theta,\boldsymbol{\Lambda}_{NT})}\end{split}\]

The different solver backends all solve this same linear system in some form.

References

[1]

Jean-Jacques Moreau. Unilateral contact and dry friction in finite freedom mechanical systems: a differential inclusion approach. In R.I. Leine, editor, Nonsmooth Mechanics and Applications, pages 1–82. Springer, 1988. URL: https://hal.science/hal-01713847/file/JJMU.pdf.
